title: How to Mix High-Hydration Sourdough Using a Wooden Spurtle
meta_description: Learn step‑by‑step how to mix high hydration sourdough with a wooden spurtle. Tips, technique, and the perfect 2‑piece wooden spurtle set for flawless dough.
html:
When you’re working with a high‑hydration sourdough, the way you combine flour, water, and starter can make the difference between a shaggy mess and a smooth, elastic dough. The secret isn’t a fancy mixer—it’s the right tool and the right method. Using a wooden spurtle to mix high hydration sourdough gives you control, reduces tearing, and keeps the dough’s delicate gluten network intact.

Why a Wooden Spurtle Beats a Spoon or Fork

A wooden spurtle is narrow, slightly tapered, and has a smooth, non‑scratch surface. That shape lets you scrape the sides of the bowl without gouging the wood, and the wood’s natural resistance to heat means you won’t melt the handle when you work with warm starter. Compared with metal forks, the spurtle’s soft edge gently folds the dough, encouraging hydration without over‑working the gluten too early.

Gather Your Tools and Ingredients

Before you start, lay out everything you’ll need. Having a clean workspace and the right utensils speeds up the process and reduces the temptation to over‑mix.

  • 2 pcs Wooden Spurtle & Sourdough Starter Stirrer Set – the skinny dough stirring stick and Danish‑style dough whisk.
  • Large glass or ceramic mixing bowl (at least 2 qt).
  • Digital kitchen scale – accuracy matters at high hydration.
  • Flour (bread or all‑purpose) and filtered water.
  • Active sourdough starter (100% hydration).
  • Bench scraper (optional, for later stages).

Step‑by‑Step: Mixing High Hydration Sourdough

1. Weigh the ingredients. For a 100 % hydration loaf, the water weight equals the flour weight. Example: 500 g flour, 500 g water, 100 g starter.

2. Combine starter and water. Dump the starter into the bowl, then add the measured water. Use the wooden spurtle to stir the mixture in a figure‑eight motion until the starter is fully dissolved. The spurtle’s slender tip reaches the bottom of the bowl, ensuring no pockets of unmixed starter remain.

3. Incorporate the flour. Sprinkle the flour over the liquid in a slow, even stream. As the flour lands, begin folding it into the wet mixture with the spurtle. The goal is to hydrate the flour without creating a dry “flour wall.” The wooden stick’s gentle pressure pulls the flour into the liquid, creating a shaggy, sticky mass.

4. Rest (autolyse). Cover the bowl and let the dough sit for 30‑45 minutes. This rest allows the flour to fully absorb the water, beginning gluten development without mechanical agitation.

5. Initial stretch and fold. After the autolyse, use the spurtle to perform a series of stretch‑and‑folds directly in the bowl. Grab the dough with the spurtle’s broad side, pull upward, and fold it over itself. Rotate the bowl and repeat 4‑5 times. The wooden surface won’t stick, making the folds smooth.

6. Bulk fermentation. Let the dough rise at room temperature, performing a set of stretch‑and‑folds every 30 minutes for the next 2‑3 hours. The spurtle remains your go‑to tool for these folds because its slim profile can navigate the sticky dough without tearing it.

7. Pre‑shape and final shaping. When the dough has roughly doubled in size, turn it onto a lightly floured surface. Use the spurtle to scrape any remaining dough from the bowl, then shape the loaf. The wooden stick can also help tighten the surface tension by gently rolling the dough under your hands.

8. Proof and bake. Place the shaped loaf in a proofing basket, cover, and let it proof until it passes the fingertip test. Transfer to a preheated oven with steam, and bake until golden brown.

Tips for Mastering the Mix

Even with the perfect tool, high‑hydration sourdough can be intimidating. These practical tips keep the process smooth:

  1. Keep the spurtle dry. After each mixing session, wipe the wooden handle clean. Excess moisture can cause the wood to swell, affecting its balance.
  2. Use a bowl with high sides. The narrow spurtle can reach the bottom without splashing, and the tall walls prevent the sticky dough from spilling.
  3. Don’t over‑mix. High hydration dough needs time to develop gluten on its own. Stop mixing once the flour is fully hydrated; the rest of the structure builds during stretch‑and‑folds.
  4. Adjust hydration gradually. If the dough feels unmanageable, reduce water by 5 g increments. The spurtle’s precise control helps you feel the dough’s texture.

Cleaning and Caring for Your Wooden Spurtle

Wooden kitchen tools last a lifetime when treated right. After each use, rinse the spurtle under warm water and scrub with a soft brush—no detergent is needed. Pat dry and rub a thin layer of food‑grade mineral oil into the wood every few weeks. This conditioning prevents cracking and keeps the surface smooth for future mixes.

FAQ

Can I use the wooden spurtle for low‑hydration doughs?

Yes. The spurtle’s tapered shape works well for any dough that needs gentle folding and scraping, from stiff rye to soft pizza dough.

Is the spurtle safe for use with acidic starters?

Absolutely. Wood is non‑reactive, so the acidity of a sourdough starter won’t affect the tool. Just rinse it promptly after use.

Do I need a separate spurtle for each batch?

No. One spurtle per household is sufficient. The 2 pcs Wooden Spurtle & Sourdough Starter Stirrer Set gives you a backup, so you always have a clean tool ready.
